coredata.dc:title: Microwave synthesis of chitosan-based carbon dots for Al3+ detection and biological application
coredata.dc:creator: Jian, Ke
Fu, Liming
Zhang, Yujie
Zhang, Hongmei
Guo, Xiangjun
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Yellow fluorescent carbon dots (Y-CDs) were prepared via microwave method using chitosan and o-phenylenediamine as the main raw materials. The obtained Y-CDs possesses good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and luminous stability. During the microwave pyrolysis carbonization process, the surface of Y-CDs was modified with the functional groups such as amino and carboxyl, which can bind to Al3+ by forming complexes, further improving the selectivity and sensitivity of the Al3+ detection. And the fluorescence of Y-CDs was quenched by Al3+ by static quenching process. More importantly, Y-CDs as fluorescent sensor was further applied for the determination of Al3+ in the real water samples with high reliability and accuracy. In addition, Y-CDs present potential application in biological imaging. The cultivated zebrafish embryos with Y-CDs displayed clearly in vivo uptake and metabolic fluorescence images, further confirming its low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave synthesis
Yellow emission
Aluminum ion
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Al3+ detection
Uptake and metabolism of Y-CDs in zebrafish

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR202110280005 ) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles ( Qingdao University ) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–b) The optimal fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis Spectra of 40-OPD, 40-MPD, 40-PPD, and 40-CTS). (c) Photos of 40-OPD, 40-MPD, 40-PPD and 40-CTS (from left to right) under 365 nm UV light (top) and sunlight (down). (d) The emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of the CDs prepared by hydrothermal method. (e) The optimal fluorescence emission spectra of 10-OPD, 20-OPD, 30-OPD, 40-OPD and 60-OPD. (f) Photos of 10-OPD, 20-OPD, 30-OPD and 40-OPD (from left to right) under 365 nm UV light (top) and sunlight (down).

(a) TEM images of the Y-CDs. (b) Size distribution histograms of the Y-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of the Y-CDs and 40-CTS. (d) Raman spectrum of the Y-CDs. (e) XRD pattern of the Y-CDs. (f) XPS survey spectrum of the Y-CDs. (g–i) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s of the Y-CDs, respectively.

(a) Absorbance (black line), fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of the Y-CDs. Inset: photographs of Y-CDs under daylight (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (b) The emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of the Y-CDs. (c) The CIE coordinates of Y-CDs. (d) pH effects on FL intensity of Y-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs adding various metal ions. (b) Fluorescence intensity change ratios F/F0 of Y-CDs adding various ions. (c) Photos under 365 nm UV light of Y-CDs without (blank) and with other ions. (d) The anti-interference ability of Y-CDs for monitoring Al3+.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs with different Al3+ concentrations (0–300 μM). The excitation wavelength was 420 nm. (b) Local magnification of fluorescence emission spectra at Al3+ concentrations of 0–10 μM. (c) The fitted curve of relative fluorescence change ratios (F0/F) versus Al3+ concentration (0–10 μM). (d) The fitted plot of F0/F versus Al3+ concentration (0–300 μM). (e) The fitted plot of linear relationship between F0/F and Al3+ concentration (10–100 μM). Inset: photographs of Y-CDs with different concentrations of Al3+ under 365 nm UV light. (f) The fluorescence quenching diagram of Y-CDs to Al3+.

(a) Zeta potential of Y-CDs in the presence of 0, 10 μM and 100 μM Al3+. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of Y-CDs in the presence of 0, 10 μM and 100 μM Al3+. (c) UV–vis spectra of Y-CDs in the presence of 0, 10 μM and 100 μM Al3+. (d) Cyclic voltammetry curve of Y-CDs. Inset: Band gap deduced from UV spectra of Y-CDs. (e) Cyclic voltammetry Y-CDs in the presence of 0, 10 μM and 100 μM Al3+. (f) Al3+ recognition mechanism based on photo-induced electron transfer.

(a–b) Distribution of Y-CDs in living zebrafish and embryos. (c) Confocal imaging of zebrafish embryos incubated with different concentrations (0, 50, 150 and 250 μg/mL) of Y-CDs culture solution for 4 h. (d) Confocal microscopy images of zebrafish embryos incubated for 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 h with 100 μg/mL of Y-CDs.
Fluorescence microscopy images of Y-CDs metabolism in zebrafish at different incubation times.
The preparation and applications of Y-CDs.

head.ce:title: Microwave synthesis of chitosan-based carbon dots for Al3+ detection and biological application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological Textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Yellow fluorescent carbon dots (Y-CDs) were prepared via microwave method using chitosan and o-phenylenediamine as the main raw materials. The obtained Y-CDs possesses good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and luminous stability. During the microwave pyrolysis carbonization process, the surface of Y-CDs was modified with the functional groups such as amino and carboxyl, which can bind to Al3+ by forming complexes, further improving the selectivity and sensitivity of the Al3+ detection. And the fluorescence of Y-CDs was quenched by Al3+ by static quenching process. More importantly, Y-CDs as fluorescent sensor was further applied for the determination of Al3+ in the real water samples with high reliability and accuracy. In addition, Y-CDs present potential application in biological imaging. The cultivated zebrafish embryos with Y-CDs displayed clearly in vivo uptake and metabolic fluorescence images, further confirming its low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Although aluminum is an important element for human health, excessive ingestion of aluminum can cause the following harms to humans and animals: adverse effects on the blood system, nervous system and bones [1], which can cause amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson's disease and human Alzheimer's disease [2]. According to the World Health Organization, the permissible level of Al3+ in drinking water is 7.4 μM [3]. As a result, the determination of the concentration of Al3+ in water environment is of great importance for the monitoring of its release and environmental pollution. The traditional detection methods include inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [4], ICP mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [6] and electrochemical methods [7]. However, these methods not only require specialized technical personnel to use precision instruments to complete, but also high detection costs and inconvenient operation [8]. Therefore, this work focuses on constructing a simple, convenient, and efficient fluorescence method for the quantitative detection of Al3+.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterials with unique fluorescence characteristics, has become a research focus in recent years because of its low toxicity, good water solubility and biocompatibility, and simple preparation process [9]. Moreover, CDs have been used in drug delivery [10], photocatalysis [11], anti-counterfeiting [12,13], and even more widely in the detection of heavy metal ions [14] and cell imaging [15]. CDs are mainly prepared via two different synthetic methods, that is, “top-down” and “bottom-up”. Significantly, microwave-assisted synthesis is a process in which energy is transferred to the reactants in a non-direct manner through microwave heating to achieve rapid and efficient synthesis of the target product [16], which not only improves the product yield, but also achieves the controllability of the reaction conditions and enhances the safety of the reaction. Therefore, microwave method, as a “bottom-up” method for preparing CDs, has been adopted due to its advantages of speed, simplicity, and environmental friendliness [17]. In addition, the introduction of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, boron, and phosphorus into the carbon skeleton can regulate the emission characteristics of CDs, thereby endowing them with different functions and optical properties [18]. Chitosan is a natural polysaccharide, as a biomacromolecule, exists in shrimp shells, crab shells, and other shellfish [19]. Due to its good biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-toxicity, and low price, it has become an important source of CDs [20]. Moreover，chitosan possesses large amount of functional groups, such as amino groups (–NH2), hydroxyl groups (–OH). It has been reported that the fluorescence properties of CDs can be improved by using chitosan as passivating agent [21]. On this basis, carbon nanotubes were prepared by self-passivation using chitosan as a carbon source without the addition of passivating agents and subsequent modifications [22]. It must be noted that the functional groups of chitosan prone to be modified on the surface of CDs during the carbonization process, this is not only beneficial for improving the optical performance of CDs, but also for improving the binding ability between CDs and the detected substances, and further improving the selectivity and sensitivity of detection. In addition, the synthesis of chitosan-based CDs provides a promising approach for the high-value utilization of biomass resources.

So far, some fluorescence sensors based on CDs have been reported for the detection Al3+. For example, Gao et al. [23] synthesized CDs with p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine using a one-pot hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained CDs was applied for the detection of Al3+, showing high selectivity for Al3+. However, the linear range was relatively narrow (0–10 μM). Alqahtani's group [24] synthesized boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN@CDs) with caffeine and H3BO3 for fluorescence sensing of Al3+ and F− based on an “on-off-on” switch. However, the BN@CDs solution needs to be dialyzed with dialysis bag (3 kDa) and then freeze-dried into a solid powder. Although these reports provided relatively satisfactory results, they were limited to complicated preparation steps, long preparation time, low detection range or highly toxic chemical materials.

In this work, CDs was synthesized by microwave assisted pyrolysis using chitosan and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials. At high microwave temperature, the long chain of chitosan breaks and binds with o-phenylenediamine, producing CDs with strong yellow fluorescence (Y-CDs). The obtained Y-CDs modified with functional groups display good water solubility and stability, as well as fluorescence quenching response to Al3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, it has the advantages of simple and fast preparation process, low cost and environmental friendliness. Additionally, zebrafish is a fish with a clear physical development process and is genetically similar to humans. In this study, zebrafish embryos cultured in Y-CDs solution were studied to investigate the fluorescence bioimaging of Y-CDs and evaluate their toxicity. The experimental process is shown in Scheme 1 .

o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Chitosan was purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Metal salts (KCl, BaCl2, AlCl3·6H2O, FeCl3, FeCl2·4H2O, MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, MnSO₄·H2O), KBr, KI, KNO3, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, Ca3(PO4)2 were purchased from Beijing chemical reagents factory (Beijing, China).
All reagents are of analytical grade and are used as received along with the chemicals without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of carbon dots were obtained using a JSM-2100PLUS microscope (JOEL, Tokyo, Japan) operating at 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). UV absorption spectra were measured using a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing General Analytical Instruments Co, Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured via KBr tablet method with the aid of Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, USA). X-ray Diffraction spectra (XRD) was obtained by a Smart Lab 3 kW X-ray Diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with an ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA). Fluorescence imaging was performed in vivo on zebrafish with a fluorescence microscope (Eclipse Ni-U, Nikon, Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence decay curves were obtained with an Edinburgh FLS980 at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. Measurement of the zeta potential of Y-CDs using the NANO-ZSE Zeta Potential Analyser (Mütek, Feldstadt, Germany). Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) curves of the samples were carried out with CHI760E (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China).

In this work, a simple microwave pyrolysis method was adopted. Firstly, 10 mg chitosan was dissolved in 10 mL 1 % acetic acid solution, then, 0.2 g o-phenylenediamine, 0.2 g m-phenylenediamine or 0.2 g p-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL chitosan-acetic acid solution respectively, and sonicated for 20 min until completely dissolved. Another 10 mL of chitosan-acetic acid solution was taken for control experiment. Then, the mixed solutions were placed in a household microwave and heated intermittently at 750 W (P70D20PL-D4) for 40 min (took out and added 10 mL water every 5 min). Notably, the solution changed to solid powder during reaction. After natural cooling, the resulting solid powder was ground and dispersed in deionized water, followed by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove precipitate. Finally, the obtained samples were named 40-OPD (p-phenylenediamine), 40-MPD (m-phenylenediamine), 40-PPD (p-phenylenediamine) and 40-CTS (control), respectively.
In contrast, CDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using 10 mL o-phenylenediamine-chitosan acetic acid solution at 220 °C for 14 h.

Furthermore, the same amount of o-phenylenediamine-chitosan acetic acid solution was heated at a high fire of 750 W microwave for 10, 20, 30, 40 and 60 min, respectively, and the obtained samples were named 10-OPD, 20-OPD, 30-OPD, 40-OPD and 60-OPD, respectively.
It must be noted that the obtained CDs with the optimal experimental condition exhibited yellow fluorescence, further named Y-CDs, and was stored at 4 °C for later experiments.

Stability tests were carried out on Y-CDs and the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs was the strongest at pH = 8. Therefore, the following experiments were carried out by adjusting the solution of Y-CDs to pH = 8.

The synthesized Y-CDs solution was used as fluorescent probe for the detection of Al3+. The selectivity of Y-CDs to Al3+ was investigated by adding various metal ions (Na+, Ca2+, K+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Mn2+) and anion ions (Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−). Y-CDs solution (600 μg/mL) was mixed thoroughly with ions at room temperature. The final concentration of each ion was 200 μM. Next, fluorescence (FL) emission spectra were recorded using λex = 420 nm with a slit width of 3 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of the Y-CDs to Al3+ and different interfering ions was analyzed. Interference experiments were carried out under the coexistent conditions with Al3+ and interference ion (100 μM).
Similarly, the sensitivity of Y-CDs to Al3+ was analyzed by adding different concentrations of Al3+ (0–300 μM).
All measurements were repeated three times to ensure the reproducibility of the method.
To evaluate the actual applicability of Y-CDs as fluorescent probe in vivo, the zebrafish imaging experiments were conducted.

Firstly, Y-CDs culture solution with concentrations of 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, and 250 μg/mL were prepared. Then, 3 mL of Y-CDs culture solutions were transferred to 24 well ELISA plate, and then 2 days old zebrafish embryos were transplanted (10 zebrafish embryos per well). After incubating for 4 h at 28 °C, the zebrafish embryos were washed with PBS three times and then were placed onto slides, and photographed with fluorescence microscopy at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm.
With the same method, zebrafish embryos were incubated in Y-CD culture solution (150 μg/mL) for different time (2, 3, 4, 5, 6 h), and then observed with fluorescence microscopy.

Furthermore, the in vivo metabolism of Y-CD was investigated. Zebrafish embryos were incubated in 100 μg/mL of Y-CD culture medium for 1 h, then removed and washed with PBS three times, and then transferred to PBS solution without Y-CDs to culture continuously. Replace the PBS solution every 12 h. Over time, the fluorescence imaging of zebrafish was monitored with fluorescence microscopy to understand the metabolism of Y-CDs in zebrafish.

CDs named 40-OPD, 40-MPD, 40-PPD were synthesized with chitosan-acetic acid solution adding o-phenylenediamin (OPD), m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD), respectively, and 40-CTS with only chitosan-acetic acid solution as control. The optimal fluorescence emission spectra are shown in Fig. 1a, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of 40-OPD is the strongest. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1b) show typical absorption peaks at about 230 nm and 290 nm for 40-OPD and 40-MPD, while wider absorption bands for 40-PPD and no significant absorption for 40-CTS. Under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, 40-OPD emits bright yellow fluorescence. While, the fluorescence intensity of 40-MPD, 40-PPD, 40-CTS is very weak (Fig. 1c).

In contrast, CDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using 10 mL o-phenylenediamine-chitosan acetic acid solution at 220 °C for 14 h. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. 1d, showing that the emission wavelength redshifted with the increase of excitation wavelength, indicating obvious excitation dependence. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength are 370 nm and 450 nm, showing blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light.

Furthermore, the effect of microwave reaction time on the prepared CDs were investigated. The same amount of o-phenylenediamine-chitosan acetic acid solution was microwave pyrolyzed for 10, 20, 30, 40 and 60 min, respectively, and the obtained CDs were named 10-OPD, 20-OPD, 30-OPD, 40-OPD and 60-OPD, respectively. The fluorescence intensity increases with the heating time of 10–40 min, then decreases significantly after 40 min, accompanied by the solution color changing from brown to light yellow (Fig. 1e&f). Therefore, the optimal experimental condition is o-phenylenediamine-chitosan acetic acid solution, microwave pyrolysis for 40 min and the obtained CDs exhibit yellow fluorescence, further named Y-CDs.

A representative TEM image and the corresponding size distribution histogram validate that the Y-CDs are spherical with a size ranging from 1 to 3 nm (Fig. 2a & b). The functional groups and chemical compositions of Y-CDs were obtained with FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2c, the broadband absorption at 3420 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of –OH and NH [25]. The peak at 1630 cm−1 is aromatic CO tensile vibration, and the peaks centered at 1490 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 are caused by the bending vibrations of CC/CN and CO groups, respectively [26]. The above results demonstrate that –OH, –COOH and CN exist on the surface of Y-CDs, and nitrogen is successfully doped in the prepared Y-CDs. These functional groups not only endow Y-CDs with extremely strong hydrophilicity, good dispersibility and stability in aqueous solution, but also interact with certain target, which is conducive to broadening the application of Y-CDs in detection. Raman spectrum was used to further identify the structural characteristics of Y-CDs. In Fig. 2d, Raman spectrum reveals two typical peaks at 1360 cm−1 (D-band) and 1530 cm−1 (G-band), which represent the sp3 carbon atoms with disordered carbon structure and sp2 bonded carbon atoms with a lattice, respectively [27]. The intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) is 0.89 (<1), further manifesting the high graphitic configuration of Y-CDs.

Fig. 2e displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Y-CDs, exhibiting a significant broad peak approximately at 47.76°, which belongs to (110) graphitized carbon [28]. The XPS spectra of Y-CDs has three characteristic peaks, which are located at 284.8 eV, 398.93 eV and 531.98 eV, respectively, indicating that Y-CDs mainly contains C, N and O, accounting for 74.76 %, 8.79 % and 16.45 %, respectively (Fig. 2f). The deconvoluted high-resolution C1s is separated into four peaks: 288.4, 286.3, 285.2 and 284.7 eV, belonging to CO, CO/CN, CC and CC, respectively (Fig. 2g) [29,30]. Two N1s peaks at 399.7 eV and 398.8 eV are assigned to pyrrolic N (CN) and pyridinic N (CNC) respectively (Fig. 2h) [31,32]. The OCO groups (533 eV), C–OH groups (532 eV) and CO groups (530.6 eV) [33,34] are presented in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2i). XPS results further prove the formation of rich functional groups such as CN, C–OH and CO on the surface of Y-CDs.

The optical properties of Y-CDs were investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Y-CDs is shown in Fig. 3a (black line), and a distinct characteristic absorption peak called E2 absorption band was found at 206 nm, resulting from the transition of the ring conjugated system of benzene ring structure. The broadband centered at 230 nm is related to the π-π* transition (CC bonds) of graphitic carbon nuclei in CDs [35]. Additionally, a characteristic absorption peak at 290 nm ascribes to the n-π* transitions (CO bonds). The Y-CDs aqueous solution exhibits bright yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Moreover, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of Y-CDs are at 420 nm and 550 nm (Fig. 3a red line and blue line), respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs were obtained by increasing the excitation wavelength from 370 to 480 nm at 10 nm intervals (Fig. 3b), showing a significant change in fluorescence intensity, but the peak position remained unchanged. This excitation-independent fluorescence property may be due to the uniformity size and surface state of Y-CDs [36]. Additionally, the Commission International de l'Eclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity coordinates of the photoluminescence (PL) was calculated to be (0.42, 0.56), which corresponds to yellow and is consistent with the emitting phenomenon observed by naked eye (Fig. 3c).

Fluorescent CDs will be used in sensing and biological fields, so it is necessary to study the stability and optimal detection conditions to realize their practical application [37]. Fig. 3d shows the effect of pH on the fluorescence properties of Y-CDs. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity first increases, up to the strongest at pH = 8, and then decreases with the increase of pH. Significantly, the fluorescence intensity has a good linear relationship with pH from 4 to 8 (the linear correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.975), indicating that Y-CDs is sensitive to pH and can be utilized for monitoring pH. The sensitivity of Y-CDs to pH is mainly attributed to the presence of many functional groups on the surface of Y-CDs, such as –NH2 and –COOH, which undergo varying degrees of protonation or deprotonation at different pH, leading to the conjugation changes between functional groups and carbon structures, further affecting the energy bands and optical performance of Y-CDs [38]. Consequently, the later sensing experiments will be performed at pH = 8.

Furthermore, the stability of Y-CDs was investigated by recoding the effects on fluorescence intensity of different batches, long-term storage, UV irradiation time, and salinity. Under the same synthesis conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs did not change significantly in different batches (Fig. S1a). Y-CDs from the same batch showed great stability after being stored at room temperature for three weeks (Fig. S1b) or irradiating with 365 nm UV light for 0–120 min (Fig. S1c). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs was no obvious changes in NaCl solutions of different concentrations (0–1.5 M), indicating that the Y-CDs could remain stable even under high sodium ion environment (Fig. S1d). This high stability can fully meet the requirements of actual detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Al3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and sensitivity are the two main indicators to reflect the sensing behavior of Y-CDs as fluorescence probes. The responsiveness of Y-CDs to metal ions was investigated by adding different metal cations (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Fe2+ and Mn2+, 200 μM). The fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs without (blank) and with different metal ions and the corresponding relative fluorescence ratio (F/F0, where F and F0 are fluorescence intensities of Y-CDs with and without ions at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, respectively) are shown in Fig. 4a and b, respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs reduces significantly only with Al3+, while slight increase when adding K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Ba2+. Compared with other interfering metal ion, Ni2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ has slightly higher quenching effect, but its quenching efficiency is <1/4 of that of Al3+. The quenching effect of Al3+on Y-CDs can be attributed to the stronger affinity between Al3+ and Y-CDs, particularly the nitrogen and oxygen functional groups in Y-CDs, which can undergo coordination reactions with Al3+, inducing electron transfer between Y-CDs and Al3+, leading to fluorescence quenching [39]. Remarkably, fluorescence quenching phenomena can be observed clearly with naked eyes (Fig. 4c). More importantly, the coexistence of interfering substance (including common cations and anions in water) present little influence on the responsiveness of Y-CDs to Al3+, demonstrating strong anti-interference ability of Y-CDs for monitoring Al3+ (Fig. 4d).

In addition to the selectivity of CDs to analytes, sensitivity is also a very important index [6]. Fig. 5a shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs at different Al3+ concentrations. In the low concentration range of 0–10 μM, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs increases slightly with the increase of Al3+ concentration. The partially enlarged image in Fig. 5b clearly displays the change in fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs under Al3+ 0–10 μM. However, when the Al3+ concentration further increases to 10–300 μM, the fluorescence intensity decreases continuously with increasing concentration, and after that, the decline trend slowed down with adding more Al3+. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of Y-CDs with Al3+ can be observed visually under 365 nm UV light, and the yellow fluorescence gradually diminished with the gradual increase of Al3+ concentration (10–100 μM) (inset of Fig. 5e).

The responsiveness of Y-CDs to Al3+ was further analyzed, and the plots of fluorescence change ratio (F0/F) vs. Al3+ concentration 10–300 μM and 0–10 μM (Fig. 5c&d) present curve relationship between them, meanwhile, two exponential functions as follows Eqs. (1) and (2) were established for 10–300 μM and 0–10 μM, respectively, indicating highly sensitivity of Y-CDs for identifying Al3+.
(1)
y
0 − 10
= 1.06
6x 0.031
(
R 2
= 0.985
(2)
y
10 − 300
= 3.578 /
1 + exp
− 0.016
x − 77.571
R 2
= 0.993

The exponential relationship between F0/F and Al3+ concentration, rather than the linear relationship reported in previous studies, indicates that this fluorescent probe possesses a higher detection sensitivity for Al3+ than other reported CDs [40]. This is may be originated from the abundant –COOH, –OH and –NH2 groups on the surface of Y-CDs, which promote the strong affinity of Y-CDs to Al3+.

It should be pointed out that there is a linear relationship between F0/F and Al3+ concentration (10–100 μM), and the fitting equation is F0/F = 0.015 [Al3+] + 0.714, with R2 is 0.984 (Fig. 5e), indicating their excellent sensing properties in the detection of Al3+. Based on 3σ/k, the lowest detection limit (LOD) was 0.2 μM. Moreover, our established method was compared with the previously reported methods for determining Al3+ (Table S1). The LOD is comparative or even lower than some other reported. The fluorescence quenching effect may result from the strong interaction of Al3+ trapped by the Y-CDs surface groups, resulting in the coordination of Y-CDs with Al3+ (Fig. 5f), forming complex [41,42]. More importantly, the practical application of Y-CDs as fluorescent sensor for determining Al3+ in real environmental water samples was performed using the standard addition method. As shown in Table S2, the recovery rates of Al3+ in tap water were 96.80 %, 90.80 %, and 103.19 %, respectively, with relative standard deviations (RSD%) of 0.33, 0.98, and 1.19, indicating that this method can provide reliable and accurate results.

Although the accurate fluorescence quenching mechanism is still unclear, it can be attributed to the intermolecular interactions between Y-CDs and quenchers, which prone to induce non-radiative electron-transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), or inner filter effect (IFE) [9,13,43]. Particularly, the nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of Y-CDs is conducive to coordinate with Al3+ to form complexes, altering the optical properties of Y-CDs. To further explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Y-CDs, Zeta potential measurements were performed to investigate the surface changes of Y-CDs. The zeta potential of Y-CDs was −7.26 mV, which changed to 5.71 and 14.2 mV when 10, 100 μM Al3+ was added, respectively (Fig. 6a). This significant change indicates that a complex reaction may occur between Y-CDs and Al3+, leading to fluorescence quenching of Y-CDs. Additionally, generally, static quenching refers to the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex between fluorescent molecules and quenchers, while dynamic quenching refers to the collision and electron transfer between the two. The most accurate method to distinguish between static quenching and dynamic quenching is the determination of fluorescence lifetime. Static quenching has a negligible effect on decay lifetime, while dynamic quenching shortens it [27]. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Y-CDs with or without Al3+ are shown in Fig. 6b, and the fluorescence decay lifetimes were estimated to a biexponential fit (Table S3) based on the following conditions. The τavg lifetimes of Y-CDs did not undergo significant attenuation after the addition of Al3+. Therefore, the quenching of Y-CDs seemed to be caused by static quenching.

Furthermore, although Y-CDs exhibited the same UV absorption peaks at 233 nm and 289 nm after adding different concentrations of Al3+ (0, 10, and 100 μM), the intensity of the UV absorption peaks reduced by the addition of Al3+ (Fig. 6c). Thus, the high selectivity of Y-CDs for Al3+ may be due to the interaction of carboxyl groups and N-containing functional groups on the surface of Y-CDs with Al3+, causing electron or energy transfer [39].

The electrochemical properties of Y-CDs were then investigated using cyclic voltammetry. The highest occupied (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied (LUMO) energy levels of Y-CDs were calculated using the following empirical equations [44]:
(3)
E HOMO
= − e
E ox
+ 4.4
(4)
E LUMO
= − e
E red
+ 4.4

Eox and Ered represent the oxidation and reduction potentials of Y-CDs, respectively. Ered was determined to be −0.31 eV by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 6d). Correspondingly, the ELUMO value was calculated to be −4.09 eV. As the oxidation process was irreversible, the Y-CDs' photo-energetic bandgap (Eg) was calculated to be 5.57 eV through the UV spectra. Subsequently, using the formula:
(5)
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
−
E g

The EHOMO of Y-CDs was estimated to be −9.66 eV. Furthermore, with the same method, the ELUMO and EHOMO of Y-CDs containing 10 μM Al3+ were −4.06 eV and −9.72 eV, respectively, and Ered and Eg were calculated to be −0.34 eV and 5.66 eV, respectively (Fig. 6e and Fig. S2a). Similarly, the ELUMO and EHOMO of Y-CDs with 100 μM Al3+ were calculated to be −4.11 eV and −10.04 eV, respectively, and Ered and Eg were −0.29 eV and 5.60 eV, respectively (Fig. S2b). When 0–10 μM Al3+ were added to Y-CDs, it may be because of the low content of Al3+, which combines with the hydroxyl group in the solution to form Al (OH)3, resulting in a slight increase in the pH value of the solution, thus enhancing its fluorescence intensity, and a slight increase in the LUMO energy level orbital. When the concentration of Al3+ increases, the amino group on the surface of Y-CDs has a stronger binding ability with Al3+, thus forming a strong coordination complex of Al3+/Y-CDs, which improves the selectivity of detection. As illustrated in Fig. 6f, when the concentration of Al3+ increases, it tends to bind to Y-CDs, promoting the transfer of excited electrons from Y-CDs' LUMO energy level to the LUMO energy level of Al3+.

Additionally, it is well known that the static quenching constant usually decreases with the increasing system temperature, while the dynamic quenching constant usually increases [45]. The corresponding fluorescence quenching constant can be calculated by the following Stern–Volmer equation:
(6)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
C
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
C
where, F0 and F are the fluorescence emission intensities of Y-CDs without and with quenching agent, respectively. Ksv and Kq are denoted as the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and the bimolecular reaction rate constant, respectively. [C] is the concentration of the quencher. τ0 is the lifetime of Y-CDs without adding the quenchers. The Stern-Volmer plots of F0/F versus Al3+ concentrations at different temperature displayed a good linear relationship (Fig. S3). The corresponding Ksv and Kq values were presented in Table S4. The quenching constant declined continuously with the increase of temperature, demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching process is a static quenching mechanism, which is agree with the decay lifetime results [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and metabolism of Y-CDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Thanks to the similarity in homologous genes between zebrafish and humans, as well as the optical transparency of their embryos and larvae, they have been regarded as a highly valuable vertebrate model and widely used in fluorescence probe research [47,48]. Zebrafishes were herein employed as the animal model to evaluate developmental biotoxicity of Y-CDs and the practicability in the bioimaging in vivo. The uptake, distribution and metabolic process of Y-CDs in zebrafishes were investigated in detail. Zebrafish embryos cultured with culture solution without adding Y-CDs (as control) did not exhibit fluorescence. However, that displayed significant chartreuse fluorescence after being incubated with Y-CDs for 4 h at 28 °C, mainly distributed in the yolk sac of zebrafish embryos (Fig. 7a). These results suggest that zebrafish embryos can uptake Y-CDs and present in vivo fluorescence imaging [49]. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity in zebrafish increased gradually as the concentration of Y-CDs increased ranging from 0 to 250 μg/mL (Fig. 7c). Moreover, the fluorescence signals also become stronger with the incubation time from 2 h to 6 h (Fig. 7d). These embryos had been developed normally in the Y-CDs solution. Remarkably, compared with control groups, they did not show significant adverse effects on zebrafish embryos with the increase of the Y-CDs concentration up to 500 μg/mL, including hatch rate (98 %), survival rate (94 %) and malformation rate (7 %). This further demonstrates the noticeable biocompatibility and low toxicity of Y-CDs, as well as enormous potential in bioimaging [50].

In particular, the distribution of Y-CDs in zebrafish was dominated by the yolk sac, showing the strongest fluorescence signal compared to other tissues, which mainly accumulate in the intestine, so these Y-CDs can enter the digestive system and be excreted with growth and development of zebrafish. The metabolic process of Y-CDs in zebrafishes was present in Fig. 8 , the fluorescence intensity of zebrafish gradually decreased with time. After 20 h, zebrafish develops from embryonic stage to juvenile stage, the fluorescence decreased significantly, even basically disappeared, indicating the complete metabolism. These results confirm that Y-CDs can served as an attractive fluorescence probe in biological imaging.

In conclusion, Y-CDs were successfully prepared using chitosan and o-phenylenediamine via microwave method. This method has the advantages of simple operation, short reaction time and low requirements for equipment. Moreover, the functional groups were modified on Y-CDs, which is related to the strong yellow fluorescence with excitation-independent behavior, as well as good water solubility and stability. The prepared Y-CDs as fluorescence sensor was further applied to the sensitive detection of Al3+ with high selectivity and anti-interference ability, offering a rapid, visual and feasible method for quantitative detection of Al3+. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be ascribed to static quenching by forming the complex between Al3+ and functional groups of Y-CDs. In addition, Y-CDs was utilized to cultivate the zebrafish embryos, displaying clear fluorescence images of uptake and metabolism in vivo, further confirming its low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, demonstrating its potential application in biological imaging.

Ke Jian: Writing – original draft. Liming Fu: Supervision, Software, Formal analysis. Yujie Zhang: Data curation. Hongmei Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiangjun Guo: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xihui Zhao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR202110280005) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815).
